blocks.content: End of October. The season turned.

Things settled into their shapes. Not resolved — just settled. Jonathan and I were talking again, real talking, even if something between us was still finding its footing. Maya and I had lunch twice and both times it was easy in a way that didn't demand anything from me yet. Keisha was still watching. Mr. Cole made dinner on Sundays like he always did. Larry Jr. drew things at the kitchen table and asked questions about everything.
Wednesday night. I was at the desk. Homework done. Nothing left to do except sit there.

Crystal was in Atlanta. I knew that the same way I knew other things I hadn't been told directly — Jonathan, gently, a Monday morning. She moved. Her family left Thursday night. I nodded like that was information and not a wall.

I didn't have her number. I didn't have anything except the steps and the way she'd laughed once and the fact that she had told Jonathan she liked me before she ever said a word to me directly. That was the whole inventory.
The AIM window was open. I looked at it for a second. No screen name to type. No address. Nothing to send anything to.
I closed the laptop.
She had something she wanted to say to me. I think about that sometimes. The version of this where I'd asked. Where she got to say it. I don't know what it was. I'll never know what it was.
Some things don't get a last line. They just stop. You carry the silence and you don't get to know what was on the other side of it.

Crystal was in Atlanta. She had been there for two weeks. I'd had one conversation with her — the real one, the long one — and I'd heard her say everything she'd been holding, and I had turned it into a question about whether it was fixable. Whether the distance was real. Whether there was a version where none of this had to be true.
I knew when it came out. The line went quiet and I knew. She hadn't called to ask me to solve it. She'd called to give me something to hold. And I'd dropped it the second it touched my hands.
Wednesday night. Desk. Homework finished. AIM open on the screen.
I checked it twice. Nothing from her.
I started typing. Got four words in. Deleted it. Started again. Deleted that too.

There was nothing I could say that didn't make it worse. Every sentence I wrote was still about me — still me reaching for something when what she had needed was for me to just be there and receive what she was giving.
I closed the laptop.

She said her heart melted every time I looked at her. She said it like a fact she'd been carrying alone for a long time. And I responded by asking if Atlanta was really real. I think about that a lot.
I went to bed. The window across the street had its light on. I stared at it until it went off.

Crystal was in Atlanta. The call had ended honest — I'd told her I didn't know what to say, and she'd heard that and accepted it and we'd said goodnight like two people who understood each other even when the words weren't there.
But I had left her holding most of it. She said everything. I said I didn't know how to answer. That was true. It was also the truth that cost something.
Wednesday night. Homework done. I heard the AIM chime and my chest did something before I even looked.
hey. just wanted to check in. i hope you're doing okay over there.
Short. Careful. She was reaching in without asking for anything back. Giving me an out if I needed one.
i am. you too i hope.
yeah. goodnight larry.
goodnight.
The chat went quiet. I stared at it for a while.

She had reached back. That meant something. But I knew what wasn't in there — the version of that conversation where I'd found the words instead of telling her I couldn't find them. She deserved the words. I'd given her honesty instead of bravery and told myself they were the same thing.
I closed the laptop. It was enough. I kept telling myself it was enough.

Crystal was in Atlanta. She was in Atlanta and her locker was somebody else's now and I still didn't know how to put it down completely. But I was carrying it differently. Less like something that had happened to me. More like something I understood.
Wednesday night. I was at the desk doing homework when the AIM chime went off.
I almost didn't look. But I looked.
hey. i know it's been a few weeks. i just wanted to say — i hope you're doing okay. i think about those steps a lot.
I sat there for a long time before I answered. The cursor blinking in the box.
There was a version of me that would have spent an hour figuring out the right thing to say. I didn't do that. I just said what was true.
i think about them too. i'm glad you called that night. really.
Three dots. Then:
me too. goodnight larry.
goodnight.
The chat went quiet. I stayed on the screen for a minute. Then I went back to homework.
That was the whole thing. And it was enough.

Crystal was in Atlanta. She was in Atlanta and her locker was somebody else's now and I still didn't know how to put it down completely. But I was carrying it differently. Less like something that had happened to me. More like something I understood.
Wednesday night. I was at the desk doing homework when the AIM chime went off.
I almost didn't look. But I looked.
hey. i know it's been a few weeks. i just wanted to say — i hope you're doing okay. i think about those steps a lot.
I sat there for a long time before I answered. The cursor blinking in the box.
I think she reached back because I hadn't filled the silence with my own need. On that call, when she said everything — I had stayed. Just stayed. She'd felt that. She was reaching toward it now.
i think about them too. i'm glad you called that night. really.
Three dots. Then:
me too. goodnight larry.
goodnight.
The chat went quiet. I stayed on the screen for a minute. Then I went back to homework.
That was the whole thing. And it was enough.
